topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the quiet afternoon. It was an email from the admissions office, a link to a recently published essay. My breath hitched in my throat as I clicked. It was *my* essay. The words, polished and eloquent, sat there, lauded as a model of introspection. I stared at the screen, my fingers trembling slightly as they hovered over the carefully crafted paragraphs. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a familiar, heavy pressure behind my ribs. I reread the opening sentence, the phrasing suddenly feeling hollow, a cheap imitation of the genuine spark I was trying to capture. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, shaky exhale. The room seemed to shrink around me, amplifying the silence. How could someone else have articulated that feeling so perfectly? It felt like a profound failure, a confirmation that my own voice was fundamentally inadequate.

[story 2]

I found the file tucked away in a forgotten folder on my desktop, a relic from a late-night drafting session. Opening it felt like picking up a mirror that showed a stranger. The essay was brilliant, undeniably so—a tapestry of nuanced experience and sharp observation. My gaze snagged on the anecdotes, the way the narrative flowed with effortless grace. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. I traced a line of text with my index finger, the paper cool beneath my touch, and a wave of intense inadequacy washed over me. Every carefully chosen adjective seemed suddenly clumsy, every insight shallow. I closed my eyes, pressing the heels of my hands against my temples, trying to physically squeeze the unwanted thoughts out. When I finally looked back, the words seemed mocking, a testament to a standard I hadn't even known existed, a standard I had demonstrably failed to meet.

[story 3]

The discovery happened during a routine file organization session. Amidst a mountain of half-finished drafts, a familiar title caught my eye—my college application essay, presented in its final, published form. My hands started to shake, a subtle tremor that I tried to mask by gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white. Reading it felt like being caught in a spotlight, exposed under harsh, unforgiving light. A deep sense of deflation settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. The narrative spoke of profound connection, of unwavering clarity, qualities that felt utterly foreign to the messy, uncertain reality of my own life. I pushed the laptop away, the sudden movement jarring. I stood up, pacing the small room, my movements jerky and undirected. Each step echoed the hollowness inside me, a relentless drumbeat of 'not enough,' vibrating through my bones.
